Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 2:

For InfiniBand specific connections, the QPs are configured with minimum RNR NAK timer and
local ACK values. The minimum RNR NAK timer value is set to 0, for a delay of 655 ms. The
local ACK timeout is calculated based on the packet lifetime and local HCA ACK delay. The
packet lifetime is determined by the InfiniBand Subnet Administrator and is part of the resolved
route (path record) information. The HCA ACK delay is a property of the locally used HCA. Retry
count and RNR retry count values are 3-bit values.

Patch Set 2:

> Patch Set 2:
> 
> For InfiniBand specific connections, the QPs are configured with minimum RNR NAK timer and
> local ACK values. The minimum RNR NAK timer value is set to 0, for a delay of 655 ms. The
> local ACK timeout is calculated based on the packet lifetime and local HCA ACK delay. The
> packet lifetime is determined by the InfiniBand Subnet Administrator and is part of the resolved
> route (path record) information. The HCA ACK delay is a property of the locally used HCA. Retry
> count and RNR retry count values are 3-bit values.

The issue is on RoCE network. We observed a delay of 4-5 min between rdma_connect and the UNREACHABLE event for a route with a router down.
This causes a QP leak because Lustre relies on ping to maintain the connection state and HCA timeout is > to kiblnd timeout.

From what I understood, the NAK retries apply when the remote node replies NACK messages. In our case the router is completely down.

All the timeouts that was able to compute are several seconds and not several minutes. So I would think that is something wrong on the network.

I will test to modify the parameters you mentioned to see how it influences the HCA timeout.

Patch Set 2:

> Patch Set 2:
> 
> > Patch Set 2:
> > 
> > For InfiniBand specific connections, the QPs are configured with minimum RNR NAK timer and
> > local ACK values. The minimum RNR NAK timer value is set to 0, for a delay of 655 ms. The
> > local ACK timeout is calculated based on the packet lifetime and local HCA ACK delay. The
> > packet lifetime is determined by the InfiniBand Subnet Administrator and is part of the resolved
> > route (path record) information. The HCA ACK delay is a property of the locally used HCA. Retry
> > count and RNR retry count values are 3-bit values.
> 
> The issue is on RoCE network.
RoCE uses an IB stack in general. Which RoCE version used - v1 or v2? Hardware or Software (rxe) ?

> We observed a delay of 4-5 min between rdma_connect and the UNREACHABLE event for a route with a router down.
It might be ARP tunable might help you. but so strange - I think main delay should be in the route resolve time. It should fill an ARP entry for destination host, but once host is resolved and ARP entry created connect should be finished without long delay, except host is down between route resolve and connect.




> This causes a QP leak because Lustre relies on ping to maintain the connection state and HCA timeout is > to kiblnd timeout.
QP leak - is different problem and should be solved separately,

Patch Set 2:

Is the problem here that QP is leaked when RDMA_CM_EVENT_UNREACHABLE is received late, or that it causes long wait on module removal?

Patch Set 2:

> Is the problem here that QP is leaked when RDMA_CM_EVENT_UNREACHABLE is received late, or that it causes long wait on module removal?

Sorry, I was not clear.

The reproducer of the LU-17480 (add route and then lustre_rmmod) will cause long wait on "lnet unconfigure". The timeout on a ping request will not trigger connection removal if we have a connection request in flight.

On server side (in production), the module removal hang forever on "lnet unconfigure" if we  do not remove all the routes before. So my hypothesis is that we leak refs somewhere if we have connection timeout > lnd_timeout/transaction_timeout. I do not have server crashdump so I cannot confirm that. But for sure, this mess up all Lustre pingers.

I found the https://www.spinics.net/lists/linux-rdma/msg105801.html. This issue matches our behavior: long timeout (>4min) for a connection request on a RoCE network. So tried to implement this to workarround this issue.

Patch Set 2:

The CEA tests to modify the retry_ount and rnr_retry_count to 1, this have no effect on the RoCE connection timeout. So hardcoded values seems to be used.

Here are the default RoCE CM timeouts:

#define CMA_CM_RESPONSE_TIMEOUT 20                         
#define CMA_MAX_CM_RETRIES 15                                
#define CMA_IBOE_PACKET_LIFETIME 16                        

To convert the timeout in us: timeout_us = 4.096 * 2^timeout

So we should have:
 packet_life = 268.435 ms
 ack_timeout = 2 * packet_life = 536.871 ms
 cm_reponse_timeout = 4294.9672 ms
 cm_reponse_timeout * max_cm_retries = 64.425 s

Patch Set 2:

In ib_send_cm_req():

cm_id_priv->timeout_ms = cm_convert_to_ms(                                 
                            param->primary_path->packet_life_time) * 2 +   
                         cm_convert_to_ms(                                 
                            param->remote_cm_response_timeout);            

timeout_ms = 4294.967 + 536.871 = 4831.838 ms
timeout_ms * max_cm_timeout = 72.5 s

Patch Set 2:

> Patch Set 2:
> 
> > Is the problem here that QP is leaked when RDMA_CM_EVENT_UNREACHABLE is received late, or that it causes long wait on module removal?
> 
> Sorry, I was not clear.
> 
> The reproducer of the LU-17480 (add route and then lustre_rmmod) will cause long wait on "lnet unconfigure". The timeout on a ping request will not trigger connection removal if we have a connection request in flight.
> 
> On server side (in production), the module removal hang forever on "lnet unconfigure" if we  do not remove all the routes before.

Should you fill a ticket about it?
it not so hard to remove all routes on shutdown, but It make fix more generic, in case other LND will be have similar problem and helps with other races shutdown vs sends.

Patch Set 2:

> Should you fill a ticket about it?

I have not enough information about it, here I report what the field observed (I do not know exactly what they have done). But it is likely to be caused by this bug.
And note, that we already remove the route in the unconfigure path.

I have updated the LU-17480:

With the following static parameters from the MOFED 5.4 (used at the CEA):

#define CMA_CM_RESPONSE_TIMEOUT 22
#define CMA_QUERY_CLASSPORT_INFO_TIMEOUT 1000
#define CMA_MAX_CM_RETRIES 15
#define CMA_CM_MRA_SETTING (IB_CM_MRA_FLAG_DELAY | 24)
#define CMA_IBOE_PACKET_LIFETIME 18
#define CMA_PREFERRED_ROCE_GID_TYPE IB_GID_TYPE_ROCE_UDP_ENCAP

We can compute the a ConnectRequest timeout to 18.4s and UNREACHABLE event timeout to 16 * 18.4 =  294.9s. This what we get with Lustre dk logs.

So to support correctly RoCE at the CEA we will need this kind of patch or to patch the MOFED.
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

parents.subject: LU-17478 clio: parallelize unaligned DIO write copy

commit.subject: LU-17480 o2iblnd: add a timeout for rdma_connect
commit.message:
LU-17480 o2iblnd: add a timeout for rdma_connect

For a RoCE network, if a RDMA connection request is sent to an
unreachable node, the CM can take >4min to return
CM_EVENT_UNREACHABLE.
This hangs lustre_rmmod if a Lustre router is down.

This patch track connection requests and apply a timeout of
lnd_timeout/4 (with a minimum of 5s) to destroy the hanging
connection.

Also, the patch decrease the timeout for
rdma_resolve_addr()/rdma_resolve_route() to 5s (like most of
the upstream drivers: sunrpc, smb).

The default timeouts should be:

lnd_timeout = (transaction_timeout - 1) / (retry_count + 1)
lnd_timeout = (150 - 1) / 3 = 49s
lnd_connreq_timeout = max(5, lnd_timeout / 4) = 12s

Test-Parameters: trivial testlist=sanity-lnet
Signed-off-by: Etienne AUJAMES <eaujames@ddn.com>
Change-Id: I09e40ffaa75424c4acca1d0cf986e1ff9c6dc96b
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/53986
Tested-by: jenkins <devops@whamcloud.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <maloo@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Chris Horn <chris.horn@hpe.com>
Reviewed-by: Frank Sehr <fsehr@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>
